central_claims: CUP (cumulative utility parity) is a novel, useful FL fairness criterion
Inverse-availability sampling + surrogate updates achieve CUP empirically
Algorithm outperforms q-FFL and PHP-FL on utility fairness metrics
strengths: Novel fairness notion: availability-normalized cumulative utility is conceptually sound and addresses a real gap
Mechanistically motivated algorithm: inverse-availability sampling and surrogate updates are principled
Real device availability traces used (Yang et al. 2021) — more realistic than synthetic
55-70% utility CV reduction vs. strong baselines

issues_found.description: No finite-sample convergence rate. Lemma 1 and Theorem 1 are asymptotic only. Appendix A Eq. 40 diverges (linear in T). Missing O(1/T^alpha) convergence rate.
Single dataset (CIFAR-10), 100 clients only. No multi-dataset validation. No scalability to 1000+ clients.
FedAvg (foundational), Ditto (personalization-based fairness), FairFedCS, FedFV all cited in related work but absent from Table 2. Without FedAvg, accuracy gains are uninterpretable.
Surrogate update contribution to fairness not isolated from inverse-availability sampling contribution.

reasoning.novelty_assessment: CUP is orthogonal to per-round fairness (q-FFL, PHP-FL) and addresses a real gap: participation heterogeneity in long-term utility. Principled and genuinely novel. Algorithm builds on standard inverse-weighting ideas but applies them to the CUP criterion.
reasoning.decision_implication: Borderline reject: novel fairness notion but theoretical contribution is incomplete without finite-sample rates. Single-dataset, small-scale experiments insufficient for ICML. Likely major revision needed.
